Esca (Black Measles or Spanish Measles).comments:
The leaf and berry symptoms may occur together in single cane or may show symptom on only one parts. The severe infestation of measles kill grapevine in a single year which is commonly called apoplexy. 
The symptoms are common in 5 to 7 year old vineyard. The prune wounds helps in pathogen entrance and establishment.
Esca (Black Measles or Spanish Measles).management: Till date there is no effective method to control this disease. Remove the infected berries, leaves and trunk and destroy them. Protect the prune wounds to minimize fungal infection using wound sealant (5% boric acid in acrylic paint) or essential oil or suitable fungicides.

Leaf blight (Isariopsis Leaf Spot).comments: Common in tropical and subtropical grapes. The disease appear late in the season. Cynthiana and Cabernet Sauvignon are susceptible to this pathogen.
Leaf blight (Isariopsis Leaf Spot).management: Fungicides sprayed for other diseases in the season may help to reduce this disease.

Powdery mildew.comments: Infection of fruit is more likely if there are rainfalls close to harvest
Powdery mildew.management: Management of powdery mildew in cherry is reliant on the application of appropriate fungicides and cultural practices which promote good air circulation around tree canopies to lower humidity

Black rot.comments: Disease favors rainy weather; spores may ooze out during rain
Black rot.management: Remove all mummified fruit from vines during dormant pruning; cultivate soil during bud break to bury mummies and reduce inoculant; application of appropriate fungicides can help control the disease

Black rot.comments: Spores can overwinter in twigs or fruit remaining on the tree and spread during rainfall.
Black rot.management: Remove dead wood, mummified fruit and cankers from trees to reduce spread of disease; burn any prunings that have been made from the tree; disease can be controlled by applying fungicides from silver tip to harvest.

Common rust.comments: Disease is spread by wind-borne spores; some of the most popularly grown sweet corn varieties have little or no resistance to the disease.
Common rust.management: The most effective method of controlling the disease is to plant resistant hybrids; application of appropriate fungicides may provide some degree on control and reduce disease severity; fungicides are most effective when the amount of secondary inoculum is still low, generally when plants only have a few rust pustules per leaf.

Northern Leaf Blight.comments: The disease mainly spread through rain splash and wind.
Northern Leaf Blight.management: Follow proper tillage to reduce fungus inoculum from crop debris. Follow crop rotation with non host crop. Grow available resistant varieties. In severe case of disease incidence apply suitable fungicide.

Bacterial spot.comments: Periods of frequent rainfall during late bloom and early petal drop increase likelihood of fruit and leaf infection; infection is rare during hot, dry weather
Bacterial spot.management: Avoid planting susceptible peach varieties in areas where disease is known; once disease is visible it can be difficult to control, protective copper applications in the Fall prior to leaf drop and/or application in early growing season may help prevent the disease; care should be taken as peach trees are very sensitive to copper

Bacterial spot.comments: Bacteria survive on crop debris; disease emergence favored by warm temperatures and wet weather; symptoms are very similar to other tomato diseases but only bacterial spot will cause a cut leaf to ooze bacterial exudate; the disease is spread by infected seed, wind-driven rain, diseased transplants, or infested soil; bacteria enter the plant through any natural openings on the leaves or any openings caused by injury to the leaves.
Bacterial spot.management: Use only certified seed and healthy transplants; remove all crop debris from planting area; do not use sprinkler irrigation, instead water from base of plant; rotate crops.

Bacterial spot.comments: Disease emergence favored by warm, wet conditions; the pathogen can be enter a field on seed and transplants and can be spread within a field by wind-driven rain, clipping plants and aerosols.
Bacterial spot.management: Use disease free planting material; remove and destroy all crop debris after harvest, or plow material deeply under soil.

Apple scab.comments: Fungus overwinters on dead foliage on ground; spores dispersed by wind; high moisture encourages fungal growth.
Apple scab.management: Remove all leaves dropped from tree in the fall and compost to prevent any diseases surviving in debris; application of zinc and fertilizer grade urea in the Fall may be necessary to speed leaf drop, lime should then be added to fallen leaves; fungicide application may be necessary in areas where leaves remain wet for periods in excess of 9 hours; fungicides such as copper soaps and Bordeaux mixture should be applied if there is a chance of wet period as soon as leaf tips emerge.

Cedar apple rust.comments: Fungus requires two hosts to complete lifecycle; forms galls on Eastern red cedar and spores are carried by wind to apple; use caution when planting apple close to red cedar.
Cedar apple rust.management: Plant resistant varieties where possible; remove nearby red cedar; if growing susceptible varieties in proximity to red cedar follow a fungicide program.

Early blight.comments: Disease can spread rapidly after plants have set fruit; movement of air-borne spores and contact with infested soil are causes for the spread of the disease
Early blight.management: Apply appropriate fungicide at first sign of disease; destroy any volunteer solanaceous plants (tomato, potato, nightshade etc); practice crop rotation

Early blight.comments: Disease overwinters in infected plant debris and soil; favored by warm temperatures and plant stress
Early blight.management: Practice crop rotation and remove infected debris; maintain proper fertilization and irrigation; apply fungicides when disease pressure is high

Septoria leaf spot.comments: Spread by water splash; fungus overwinters in plant debris.
Septoria leaf spot.management: Ensure all tomato crop debris is removed and destroyed in Fall or plowed deep into soil; plant only disease-free material; avoid overhead irrigation; stake plants to increase air circulation through the foliage; apply appropriate fungicide if necessary.

Late blight.management: Plant resistant varieties; if signs of disease are present or if rainy conditions are likely or if using overhead irrigation appropriate fungicides should be applied.

Late blight.comments: Pathogen survives on infected tubers and volunteer plants; disease spreads rapidly during cool, wet weather
Late blight.management: Use certified disease-free seed potatoes; destroy volunteer plants and cull piles; apply protective fungicides preventatively during favorable conditions

Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl disease.comments: The virus is transmitted by white flies and may cause 100 % yield loss if the plants infect at early stage of crop. The virus also infect other hosts like common bean, ornamental plants and several weed species.
Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl disease.management: Grow available resistant varieties. Transplant only disease and whiteflies free seedlings. Remove the infected plants and burn them. Keep the field free from weeds. Use yellow sticky traps to monitor and control whiteflies. If the insect infestation is severe spray suitable insecticides.

Leaf scorch.comments: Disease emergence favored by wet foliage for extended periods of time.
Leaf scorch.management: Plant resistant varieties; regular renewal of plants; plant in an area with good air circulation and drainage in full sun; remove all foliage from plants at harvest; application of appropriate foliar fungicide may be required to provide control.

Cercospora leaf spot.comments: A foliar disease that produces rectangular, gray to tan lesions on leaves and can significantly reduce yield by limiting photosynthesis.
Cercospora leaf spot.management: Use of preventative management tactics—such as using a corn product that is less susceptible to gray leaf spot, practicing crop rotation, removing corn residue, or using tillage to promote the decomposition of corn debris—can help reduce the impact of gray leaf spot in high-risk fields.
